name: General Knowledge
description: A grab bag of geography, culture, literature, and random facts

questions.q: What is the smallest country in the world by area?

source.notes: ~0.44 km² (110 acres). Enclosed within Rome.

questions.q: Which U.S. city is commonly known as 'The Windy City'?

source.notes: Nickname predates 1900; origin debated (politics vs weather).

questions.q: New York City's most famous nickname is what piece of fruit?

source.notes: Popularized by NYC tourism campaign in the 1970s.

questions.q: Which U.S. state is nicknamed the 'Lone Star State'?

source.notes: References the single star on the Texas flag, symbolizing independence.

questions.q: What is the largest hot desert in the world?

source.notes: ~9.2 million km². Antarctica is larger but is a cold desert.

questions.q: Lesotho is unusual because it is entirely surrounded by which other country?

source.notes: One of only three countries completely enclosed by another (with Vatican City and San Marino).

questions.q: Argentina gets its name from the Latin word 'argentum,' which refers to which element?

source.notes: Spanish explorers expected to find silver there.

questions.q: Which country's flag features an eagle perched on a cactus eating a serpent?

source.notes: Based on an Aztec legend about founding Tenochtitlan.

questions.q: As of 2024, how many member states are in the European Union?

source.notes: 28 before Brexit (UK left Jan 2020). Croatia was the last to join (2013).

questions.q: In which city was the RMS Titanic built?

source.notes: Built by Harland and Wolff. Launched May 31, 1911.

questions.q: Despite its name, the famous Spanish Riding School is located in which European capital?

source.notes: 'Spanish' refers to Spanish horse bloodlines. Founded 1572.

questions.q: The Viennese dish Wiener Schnitzel takes its name from which city?

source.notes: 'Wiener' = Viennese. Traditionally made with veal.

questions.q: The Blue Nile and White Nile converge to form the Nile in which African capital city?

source.notes: Capital of Sudan. The confluence is called al-Mogran.

questions.q: The city of Reims is at the heart of which famous French wine-producing region?

source.notes: Reims Cathedral is where French kings were crowned.

questions.q: The tango, a passionate partner dance, originated in which South American country?

source.notes: Developed in Buenos Aires in the late 19th century.

questions.q: Luzon is the largest and most populous island in which country made up of over 7,000 islands?

source.notes: Manila, the capital, is on Luzon.

questions.q: Walt Disney's famous mouse was nearly given which different first name before 'Mickey' was chosen?

source.notes: Legend says Walt's wife Lillian suggested Mickey instead.

questions.q: Who wrote 'Frankenstein,' first published in 1818?

source.notes: Full title: Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus. Shelley was 20 when published.

questions.q: Who wrote the mystery novel 'Murder on the Orient Express'?

source.notes: Published 1934. Features detective Hercule Poirot.

questions.q: Who wrote 'To Kill a Mockingbird'?

source.notes: Published 1960. Won the Pulitzer Prize in 1961.

questions.q: Which poet and civil rights activist wrote the memoir 'I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings'?

source.notes: Published 1969. First of seven autobiographies.

questions.q: The opening line 'Call me Ishmael' comes from which classic American novel?

source.notes: By Herman Melville, published 1851.

questions.q: The self-governing Faroe Islands are a territory of which Scandinavian country?

source.notes: 18 islands between Norway and Iceland. Population ~54,000.

questions.q: 'Baile Átha Cliath' is the name in Irish for which European capital city?

source.notes: Means 'town of the hurdled ford.' Dublin derives from Dubh Linn ('black pool').

questions.q: In which Italian city would you find the Bridge of Sighs and a network of famous canals?

source.notes: Bridge of Sighs (Ponte dei Sospiri) connects the Doge's Palace to the prison.
